verses.text: And David fled from Naioth in Ramah, and came and said before Jonathan, What have I done? what is mine iniquity? and what is my sin before thy father, that he seeketh my life? Guidance: David has slipped out of Naioth while Saul lay prophesying naked before Samuel, and his first stop is Jonathan. The questions tumble out in triples: 'What have I done? What is mine iniquity? What is my sin?' Three different Hebrew words for wrongdoing — the act, the moral category, the offense. David is asking Jonathan, his closest friend at court, to do the self-examination David himself cannot do alone: is there anything in me that warrants this? The chapter starts with a man not assuming his own innocence; David wants to be sure he is not earning the king's hatred. The honest examination of one's own conduct is the first thing a faithful person owes in any conflict — even when the other party is plainly in the wrong. Jonathan, given the opening, will speak the truth David needs to hear.

And he said unto him, God forbid; thou shalt not die: behold, my father will do nothing either great or small, but that he will shew it me: and why should my father hide this thing from me? it is not so. Guidance: Jonathan's reflex is filial loyalty: 'God forbid; thou shalt not die.' He believes — wants to believe — that he is fully in his father's confidence on matters of state, and therefore Saul cannot be planning anything Jonathan doesn't know about. The reasoning is structurally sound but emotionally rosy. Jonathan does not yet see that the chapter 19 conversation in which Saul swore not to kill David and the present moment are connected — that Saul has been keeping his current intent specifically from the son who would oppose it. Sometimes the people closest to a deteriorating leader are the last to see how deep the deterioration has gone, because they have a vested interest in the leader being who they were. Faithful friendship sometimes requires correcting that hopefulness gently — which is what David is about to do.

And David sware moreover, and said, Thy father certainly knoweth that I have found grace in thine eyes; and he saith, Let not Jonathan know this, lest he be grieved: but truly as the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth, there is but a step between me and death. Guidance: David doesn't argue Jonathan's hopeful theory; he diagnoses it. 'Thy father certainly knoweth that I have found grace in thine eyes; and he saith, Let not Jonathan know this, lest he be grieved.' David has seen what Jonathan has not: Saul has learned not to mention the David problem in his son's presence. The information silence is the very evidence of the murder intent. Then David's oath — 'as the LORD liveth, and as thy soul liveth' — both the highest and most intimate oath forms in Israel — 'there is but a step between me and death.' One of the most poignant lines in scripture. David is not exaggerating; he was twice within javelin-range, once with a death squad outside his door. He has measured the distance to his own grave in single paces. There is a kind of clarity that only comes when you can see your own death from where you stand; David has it and Jonathan needs it.

Then said Jonathan unto David, Whatsoever thy soul desireth, I will even do it for thee. Guidance: One sentence, full surrender. Jonathan does not finish arguing; he simply accepts David's assessment and offers a blank check. 'Whatsoever thy soul desireth, I will even do it for thee.' He gives David the right to set the agenda. The willingness to be the one helping rather than the one centered is the watermark of true loyalty. Notice that Jonathan does not insist on debating whether David's danger is real; he trusts David's reading more than his own family's narrative. Faith in a friend, when the friend has consistently shown character, can override the natural pull of family loyalty. This single sentence is the hinge that makes the whole rest of the chapter possible.

And David said unto Jonathan, Behold, to morrow is the new moon, and I should not fail to sit with the king at meat: but let me go, that I may hide myself in the field unto the third day at even. If thy father at all miss me, then say, David earnestly asked leave of me that he might run to Bethlehem his city: for there is a yearly sacrifice there for all the family. If he say thus, It is well; thy servant shall have peace: but if he be very wroth, then be sure that evil is determined by him. Guidance: The new moon (rosh chodesh) was the Hebrew first-of-month feast: a sacrificial meal at court, attendance expected from senior officials and the king's son-in-law. David proposes a clean test. He will skip the feast and hide in the field; Jonathan will offer a plausible cover (Bethlehem family sacrifice — yearly clan-sacrifices were genuine and well-known); Saul's reaction will function as a diagnostic. Mild displeasure means David's life is safe; fury means murder is determined. David is not asking Jonathan to defend him verbatim; he is asking him to read the king's eyes. The plan is wisdom under threat: not paranoia, not denial, but a controlled experiment with an exit strategy if the result is bad. Faith is sometimes paired with operational discernment; trusting God for one's life does not mean failing to set up the most informative possible test.

Therefore thou shalt deal kindly with thy servant; for thou hast brought thy servant into a covenant of the LORD with thee: notwithstanding, if there be in me iniquity, slay me thyself; for why shouldest thou bring me to thy father? Guidance: 'Deal kindly with thy servant' — the verb chesed, covenant-faithful love. David appeals to the formal bond between him and Jonathan (the covenant of 18:3-4) as the basis for asking the favor; he is not relying on personal sentiment but on covenant obligation. Then a remarkable line: 'if there be in me iniquity, slay me thyself; for why shouldest thou bring me to thy father?' David is saying: if I am the one in the wrong, you do it — don't subcontract it to your father. Two things to read here. (1) David is so confident of his innocence that he is offering Jonathan the right to kill him on demand. (2) David is sparing Jonathan the loyalty conflict — he would rather die at the hand of a friend who could be sure he deserved it than at the hand of a king who hated him without cause. The line is theatrical but also true. Sometimes the deepest gesture of trust is to invite your closest person to be your judge.

And Jonathan said, Far be it from thee: for if I knew certainly that evil were determined by my father to come upon thee, then would not I tell it thee? Then said David to Jonathan, Who shall tell me? or what if thy father answer thee roughly? And Jonathan said unto David, Come, and let us go out into the field. And they went out both of them into the field. Guidance: Jonathan recoils at the suggestion that he would kill David — 'far be it from thee' — and reassures: 'if I knew certainly… would I not tell thee?' He has now accepted David's diagnostic plan; what remains is the mechanism for getting word out to David once Jonathan knows the answer. David's question is operational: 'Who shall tell me?' (i.e., who will be the courier; if Saul is watching, a public messenger is dangerous.) Jonathan's reply skips the courier question and elevates the conversation: 'come, let us go out into the field.' The most consequential vow of the chapter is about to be made not in the palace but in the open air, away from listening ears. Important conversations sometimes need open ground, where no walls are between two people and God.

And Jonathan said unto David, O LORD God of Israel, when I have sounded my father about to morrow any time, or the third day, and, behold, if there be good toward David, and I then send not unto thee, and shew it thee; The LORD do so and much more to Jonathan: but if it please my father to do thee evil, then I will shew it thee, and send thee away, that thou mayest go in peace: and the LORD be with thee, as he hath been with my father. Guidance: Out in the open field, Jonathan swears the covenant verbally and theologically. 'O LORD God of Israel' — direct address to God as covenant witness. The structure is bilateral: 'if good… and I do not tell thee' (self-curse for failure to communicate good news) and 'if evil… I will shew it' (commitment to communicate bad news). Either way, Jonathan promises that David will know the truth. Then a blessing of breathtaking generosity: 'the LORD be with thee, as he hath been with my father.' Jonathan invokes for David exactly the divine presence that has departed from Saul — invoking it knowing what it will mean. He is, in this single sentence, blessing David's ascent and accepting his own family's eclipse. He sees what is happening and he chooses to bless it. There is a kind of holiness that gives away what cannot be kept and is more beautiful for the giving.

And thou shalt not only while yet I live shew me the kindness of the LORD, that I die not: But also thou shalt not cut off thy kindness from my house for ever: no, not when the LORD hath cut off the enemies of David every one from the face of the earth. So Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David, saying, Let the LORD even require it at the hand of David's enemies. Guidance: Jonathan now makes an ask of his own, but it is humble and forward-looking. 'Not only while I live shew me kindness… but also not cut off thy kindness from my house for ever.' He is asking David not to wipe out the Saulide royal line after the kingdom transfers — a standard practice for new dynasties replacing old ones. The plea is not for his own life only; it is for his children. (David will keep this vow in 2 Samuel 9, when he brings Mephibosheth — Jonathan's son — to his own table; the covenant runs forward two generations.) Then Jonathan formalizes the vow: 'Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David.' Note the directionality: not 'with David,' but 'with the house of David' — Jonathan is binding his line to a dynasty he is now actively passing the throne to. The closing line — 'let the LORD even require it at the hand of David's enemies' — is a self-curse against David in case he ever breaks the covenant; it invokes divine vengeance on David's adversaries if David himself ever proves an adversary to Jonathan's house. The covenant is two-way and watertight.

And Jonathan caused David to swear again, because he loved him: for he loved him as he loved his own soul. Guidance: 'Jonathan caused David to swear again, because he loved him.' The repeated oath is for Jonathan's emotional reassurance, not for legal weight. Jonathan asked for the additional swearing because of love — he needs to hear the words again to settle his heart. The narrator repeats the love-statement: 'he loved him as he loved his own soul.' The very phrase from 18:1, now reappearing at the seal of the covenant. Love is the engine; the covenant is its form. The chapter is showing us that the deepest human bonds are made in two layers — the actual love underneath, and the spoken, witnessed, sworn structure on top — neither sufficient on its own. The chapter could have ended here; everything that follows is the operational execution of the bond already made.

Then Jonathan said to David, To morrow is the new moon: and thou shalt be missed, because thy seat will be empty. And when thou hast stayed three days, then thou shalt go down quickly, and come to the place where thou didst hide thyself when the business was in hand, and shalt remain by the stone Ezel. Guidance: Now the operational details. The Hebrew feast of new moon traditionally ran two days because of the uncertainty of when the moon would actually become visible — hence David's three-day plan covers the whole event plus the day after. 'The place where thou didst hide thyself when the business was in hand' refers to David's earlier hiding spot, location unspecified but known to both. 'The stone Ezel' (or 'the departing stone' — the Hebrew is uncertain) was probably a prominent boundary marker visible from the field. The detail is exact because the operation depends on it. Two friends arranging a covert meet-point in a country where the head of state wants one of them dead — they need precision. Faithfulness in dangerous times keeps its appointments down to the rock.

And I will shoot three arrows on the side thereof, as though I shot at a mark. And, behold, I will send a lad, saying, Go, find out the arrows. If I expressly say unto the lad, Behold, the arrows are on this side of thee, take them; then come thou: for there is peace to thee, and no hurt, as the LORD liveth. But if I say thus unto the young man, Behold, the arrows are beyond thee; go thy way: for the LORD hath sent thee away. And as touching the matter which thou and I have spoken of, behold, the LORD be between thee and me for ever. Guidance: The famous arrow code. Jonathan will go to a field with a small servant boy and three arrows. He will shoot the arrows past a target as if for casual practice. The boy will be sent to retrieve them. The verbal instruction Jonathan gives the boy will, unbeknownst to the boy, also be the signal to David, hidden nearby. 'On this side of thee' (boy stop short) means safe; 'beyond thee' (boy run further) means flee. The brilliance of the code is that the boy is the message-carrier without knowing he is the message; if Saul's spies are watching, all they see is archery practice. The closing line — 'the LORD be between thee and me for ever' — reasserts the covenant frame around the operational detail. Even the cleverest tactic should be wrapped in remembering Whose presence keeps the bond.

So David hid himself in the field: and when the new moon was come, the king sat him down to eat meat. And the king sat upon his seat, as at other times, even upon a seat by the wall: and Jonathan arose, and Abner sat by Saul's side, and David's place was empty. Nevertheless Saul spake not any thing that day: for he thought, Something hath befallen him, he is not clean; surely he is not clean. Guidance: First day of the feast. David is hidden. The royal table has its usual arrangement: Saul against the wall (a habitual safety position — back to the wall, sight-lines covered, javelin within reach); Jonathan stands as the chapter's text says (perhaps to be ready to serve or to mediate); Abner the commander sits beside Saul; David's seat is empty. Saul does not comment that day. The text gives us his thought: David must be ritually unclean from some minor contact (touching a corpse, an emission, contact with an unclean animal) and therefore unable to attend until evening purification. The same Saul who had abandoned ritual order in chapters 13-15 now extends ritual-purity benefit-of-the-doubt to David. The silence is significant — Saul has, for one day, accepted a plausible religious explanation for David's absence. Even men nursing murder can occasionally pause for ceremony. The pause will not survive day two.

And it came to pass on the morrow, which was the second day of the month, that David's place was empty: and Saul said unto Jonathan his son, Wherefore cometh not the son of Jesse to meat, neither yesterday, nor to day? And Jonathan answered Saul, David earnestly asked leave of me to go to Bethlehem: And he said, Let me go, I pray thee; for our family hath a sacrifice in the city; and my brother, he hath commanded me to be there: and now, if I have found favour in thine eyes, let me get away, I pray thee, and see my brethren. Therefore he cometh not unto the king's table. Guidance: Day two. The seat is still empty; the cleanliness explanation has expired. Saul opens with the slur he has already begun using: 'the son of Jesse' — refusing to use David's name, reducing him to his rural family lineage. Jonathan delivers the prepared cover story exactly as planned. He even adds a humanizing detail (David's older brother specifically commanded him to attend) to make the alibi feel granular and authentic. Jonathan tells the lie they had pre-agreed; he does not improvise. In the moment of confrontation, sticking to the script protects everyone. He frames the request with deference — 'if I have found favour in thine eyes' — using the courtly form. The performance is convincing; the experiment David designed is about to deliver its diagnostic.

And it came to pass on the morrow, which was the second day of the month, that David's place was empty: and Saul said unto Jonathan his son, Wherefore cometh not the son of Jesse to meat, neither yesterday, nor to day? And Jonathan answered Saul, David earnestly asked leave of me to go to Bethlehem: And he said, Let me go, I pray thee; for our family hath a sacrifice in the city; and my brother, he hath commanded me to be there: and now, if I have found favour in thine eyes, let me get away, I pray thee, and see my brethren. Therefore he cometh not unto the king's table. Guidance: Day two. The seat is still empty; the cleanliness explanation has expired. Saul opens with the slur he has already begun using: 'the son of Jesse' — refusing to use David's name, reducing him to his rural family lineage. Jonathan delivers the prepared cover story exactly as planned. He even adds a humanizing detail (David's older brother specifically commanded him to attend) to make the alibi feel granular and authentic. Jonathan tells the lie they had pre-agreed; he does not improvise. In the moment of confrontation, sticking to the script protects everyone. He frames the request with deference — 'if I have found favour in thine eyes' — using the courtly form. The performance is convincing; the experiment David designed is about to deliver its diagnostic.

And Jonathan answered Saul, David earnestly asked leave of me to go to Bethlehem: And he said, Let me go, I pray thee; for our family hath a sacrifice in the city; and my brother, he hath commanded me to be there: and now, if I have found favour in thine eyes, let me get away, I pray thee, and see my brethren. Therefore he cometh not unto the king's table. Guidance: Day two. The seat is still empty; the cleanliness explanation has expired. Saul opens with the slur he has already begun using: 'the son of Jesse' — refusing to use David's name, reducing him to his rural family lineage. Jonathan delivers the prepared cover story exactly as planned. He even adds a humanizing detail (David's older brother specifically commanded him to attend) to make the alibi feel granular and authentic. Jonathan tells the lie they had pre-agreed; he does not improvise. In the moment of confrontation, sticking to the script protects everyone. He frames the request with deference — 'if I have found favour in thine eyes' — using the courtly form. The performance is convincing; the experiment David designed is about to deliver its diagnostic.

Then Saul's anger was kindled against Jonathan, and he said unto him, Thou son of the perverse rebellious woman, do not I know that thou hast chosen the son of Jesse to thine own confusion, and unto the confusion of thy mother's nakedness? For as long as the son of Jesse liveth upon the ground, thou shalt not be established, nor thy kingdom. Wherefore now send and fetch him unto me, for he shall surely die. Guidance: The mask comes off. Saul does not buy the Bethlehem story for a moment; in fact, he correctly diagnoses the conspiracy: 'thou hast chosen the son of Jesse to thine own confusion.' The insult is brutal: 'thou son of the perverse rebellious woman' — Saul slanders his own wife (Ahinoam, Jonathan's mother) to wound his son. 'The confusion of thy mother's nakedness' is sexual shaming language, deeply degrading in Hebrew idiom. Then Saul names what he has been thinking but never said: 'as long as the son of Jesse liveth upon the ground, thou shalt not be established, nor thy kingdom.' He sees the dynastic threat clearly. The verse is a confession dressed as rage — Saul knows the throne is going to David, and Jonathan's love for David is the family's surrender of it. 'Send and fetch him unto me, for he shall surely die.' Saul drops all pretense; he is asking his own son to bring David to be murdered. The experiment David designed has delivered its verdict in three sentences.

For as long as the son of Jesse liveth upon the ground, thou shalt not be established, nor thy kingdom. Wherefore now send and fetch him unto me, for he shall surely die. 'The confusion of thy mother's nakedness' is sexual shaming language, deeply degrading in Hebrew idiom. 'Send and fetch him unto me, for he shall surely die.' Saul drops all pretense; he is asking his own son to bring David to be murdered. And Jonathan answered Saul his father, and said unto him, Wherefore shall he be slain? what hath he done? And Saul cast a javelin at him to smite him: whereby Jonathan knew that it was determined of his father to slay David. Guidance: Jonathan, instead of obeying or backing down, asks the exact question that exposes the absence of a real charge: 'wherefore shall he be slain? what hath he done?' It is the same question Pilate will later ask about Jesus — a judicial demand for a stated crime that no one can name. The answer Saul gives is not words but a javelin. He hurls his spear at his own son. Saul has now thrown spears at three people: David twice (18:11), Jonathan once (here). The progression is total — the king's spear no longer respects family, friend, or covenant. 'Jonathan knew that it was determined of his father to slay David.' He needed no more evidence. The diagnostic test is complete in the worst possible way; David's reading of Saul's heart was exactly correct, and Saul has just demonstrated the same lethal intent toward his own crown prince. A father willing to spear his son cannot be trusted with anyone.

The diagnostic test is complete in the worst possible way; David's reading of Saul's heart was exactly correct, and Saul has just demonstrated the same lethal intent toward his own crown prince. A father willing to spear his son cannot be trusted with anyone.

So Jonathan arose from the table in fierce anger, and did eat no meat the second day of the month: for he was grieved for David, because his father had done him shame. Guidance: Jonathan walks out of the feast. 'In fierce anger' — the Hebrew is hot rage. He fasts the rest of the day. The narrator gives us the reason: 'he was grieved for David, because his father had done him shame.' Notice that Jonathan's grief is *for David*, not for himself. His own father has just thrown a javelin at his head, and Jonathan's emotional center is still his absent friend. This is what love-as-himself looks like in practice: when an outrage is committed against you and your friend in the same gesture, your sorrow is for the friend. Jonathan, the king's son, fasts in protest of his own father's behavior. There is no higher form of family loyalty than refusing to be loyal to the family's sin. Sometimes the way to honor a father is to publicly grieve his decisions.

And it came to pass in the morning, that Jonathan went out into the field at the time appointed with David, and a little lad with him. And he said unto his lad, Run, find now the arrows which I shoot. And as the lad ran, he shot an arrow beyond him. And when the lad was come to the place of the arrow which Jonathan had shot, Jonathan cried after the lad, and said, Is not the arrow beyond thee? Guidance: At the appointed hour, Jonathan goes to the field with a small servant boy and a bow. He shoots arrows past the boy and calls the prearranged signal: 'Is not the arrow beyond thee?' The phrase carries two meanings — to the boy it is a hurry-up; to David, hidden within earshot, it is the verdict on Saul's heart. The narrator slows down here because both audiences are processing the same words differently. Jonathan does not need to look at David; the speech does the work. There is a particular grace in this verse — Jonathan has agreed to do this for David and is doing it cleanly, even though the act itself is also Jonathan's own father's funeral. The protective friendship is being performed in plain sight while the king's house collapses in private. God can grant the steadiness to do the right thing for a friend on the day your family fell apart.

And it came to pass in the morning, that Jonathan went out into the field at the time appointed with David, and a little lad with him. And he said unto his lad, Run, find now the arrows which I shoot. And as the lad ran, he shot an arrow beyond him. The narrator slows down here because both audiences are processing the same words differently. Jonathan does not need to look at David; the speech does the work. The protective friendship is being performed in plain sight while the king's house collapses in private. God can grant the steadiness to do the right thing for a friend on the day your family fell apart.

And Jonathan cried after the lad, Make speed, haste, stay not. And Jonathan's lad gathered up the arrows, and came to his master. But the lad knew not any thing: only Jonathan and David knew the matter. Guidance: Jonathan adds 'make speed, haste, stay not' — emphatic urgency. To the boy, it is a 'pick up the arrows and let's go' command; to David, it is the order to flee fast and not delay. The narrator gives us the explicit interpretive key: 'the lad knew not any thing: only Jonathan and David knew the matter.' Sometimes God's most consequential rescues happen with bystanders entirely unaware of what they have just been part of. The boy ran a normal errand on a normal day; he never knew his master had used him as a courier between two covenant-friends to save the LORD's anointed from execution. Many of us, looking back, will discover that we were the bystander boy in someone else's deliverance. We carry messages whose meaning we do not yet read.

Many of us, looking back, will discover that we were the bystander boy in someone else's deliverance. We carry messages whose meaning we do not yet read.

And Jonathan gave his artillery unto his lad, and said unto him, Go, carry them to the city. And as soon as the lad was gone, David arose out of a place toward the south, and fell on his face to the ground, and bowed himself three times: and they kissed one another, and wept one with another, until David exceeded. Guidance: The signal sent, Jonathan dismisses the boy back to town with the bow and quiver ('artillery' is the KJV's archaic word for missile-equipment, not artillery in the modern sense). With the field cleared, David emerges from his hiding place — 'a place toward the south,' specific enough that they had agreed it beforehand. He bows three times — the formal honors due a king. The chapter is showing us that David, even as fugitive, treats Jonathan as royalty. Then the bow ends and the embrace begins: 'they kissed one another, and wept one with another, until David exceeded.' The Hebrew is striking: David wept more than Jonathan. The future king, who will weep when his enemies die later, weeps hardest now when his friend is alive in front of him. He has just lost everything else in his life — his wife, his job, his court, his country — and he is now losing the one human relationship that had sustained him through it. The tears are proportional to the loss. Sometimes the depth of one's love is most visible in the moment of forced parting.

With the field cleared, David emerges from his hiding place — 'a place toward the south,' specific enough that they had agreed it beforehand. He bows three times — the formal honors due a king. The chapter is showing us that David, even as fugitive, treats Jonathan as royalty. Then the bow ends and the embrace begins: 'they kissed one another, and wept one with another, until David exceeded.' The Hebrew is striking: David wept more than Jonathan. The future king, who will weep when his enemies die later, weeps hardest now when his friend is alive in front of him. The tears are proportional to the loss. Sometimes the depth of one's love is most visible in the moment of forced parting.

And Jonathan said to David, Go in peace, forasmuch as we have sworn both of us in the name of the LORD, saying, The LORD be between me and thee, and between my seed and thy seed for ever. And he arose and departed: and Jonathan went into the city. Guidance: 'Go in peace.' The most ancient and tender Hebrew farewell — lekh leshalom — wishes wholeness for the road ahead. Jonathan does not minimize the danger; he blesses David into it. He grounds the parting in the only thing that survives the parting: 'we have sworn both of us in the name of the LORD.' The covenant is what now carries the relationship; their physical presence to each other will be over (they will meet only once more, briefly, in the wilderness in chapter 23). 'The LORD be between me and thee, and between my seed and thy seed for ever.' The promise that began in v14 is restated as the seal. Then the verbs of parting: 'he arose and departed: and Jonathan went into the city.' One walks toward the wilderness, the other walks back to a court that just tried to kill them both. The chapter ends with the two friends going in opposite physical directions while remaining bound forever in covenant. Some farewells are not endings; they are simply the shape that a permanent love has to take when the world will no longer allow proximity. The next chapter begins David's wilderness years. Jonathan will fight beside Saul to the death on Gilboa, and David will mourn for him with one of the most beautiful elegies in scripture. The friendship in this chapter is one of the great gifts of God to His people: the picture that even when the political world cannot hold a relationship, the covenant can.
